core_meaning: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える means something is visible or can be seen — it describes the spontaneous, passive act of something appearing in your field of vision, not the deliberate act of looking. It also means 'to seem' or 'to look like' when describing how something appears to you.

sentences.jp: あ、あそこに<ruby>富士山<rt>ふじさん</rt></ruby>が<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>えるよ！
sentences.translation: Oh, you can see Mt. Fuji over there!
sentences.notes: Classic use: something comes into view spontaneously — you didn't try to see it, it's just visible.

sentences.jp: <ruby>霧<rt>きり</rt></ruby>が<ruby>濃<rt>こ</rt></ruby>くて、<ruby>前<rt>まえ</rt></ruby>の<ruby>車<rt>くるま</rt></ruby>がほとんど<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>えなかった。
sentences.translation: The fog was so thick I could barely see the car in front of me.
sentences.notes: Shows the negative form — something that should be visible isn't, due to conditions.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼女<rt>かのじょ</rt></ruby>は<ruby>年齢<rt>ねんれい</rt></ruby>より<ruby>若<rt>わか</rt></ruby>く<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える。
sentences.translation: She looks younger than her age.
sentences.notes: The 'seem / look like' sense — describes how someone or something appears to the observer.

sentences.jp: この<ruby>問題<rt>もんだい</rt></ruby>は<ruby>簡単<rt>かんたん</rt></ruby>に<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>えるが、<ruby>実<rt>じつ</rt></ruby>は<ruby>非常<rt>ひじょう</rt></ruby>に<ruby>複雑<rt>ふくざつ</rt></ruby>だ。
sentences.translation: This problem looks simple, but it is actually extremely complex.
sentences.notes: Common written pattern: Xに<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>えるが、<ruby>実<rt>じつ</rt></ruby>は… — 'appears to be X, but in reality…'

sentences.jp: ここからだと<ruby>星<rt>ほし</rt></ruby>がよく<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>えるから、<ruby>夜<rt>よる</rt></ruby>また<ruby>来<rt>き</rt></ruby>よう。
sentences.translation: You can see the stars really well from here, so let's come back tonight.
sentences.notes: Shows <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える combined with an adverb (よく) to describe quality of visibility.

dont_use: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える should not replace <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る when you mean a deliberate act of looking or watching — say テレビを<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る (watch TV), not テレビが<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える. Also avoid confusing it with <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる (to show something to someone) — learners sometimes mix these up. Finally, don't use <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える for abstract 'understanding' the way English uses 'I see' — use なるほど or わかる instead.

confused_with.contrast: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>られる is the potential form of <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る — it means 'to be able to see (something) if you make the effort,' while <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える means something is simply visible without any effort. For example, <ruby>映画<rt>えいが</rt></ruby>が<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>られる means 'I can watch the movie (I have the means/permission),' whereas <ruby>映画<rt>えいが</rt></ruby>が<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える would mean the screen is physically visible from where you are. Use <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える when visibility is the point; use <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>られる when ability or opportunity is the point.

frequency: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える is very common in daily speech, appearing naturally in conversation, news, and writing. The 'look like / seem' sense (Xに<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える、Xく<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える) is especially frequent in both spoken and written Japanese at all levels.
anki_hint: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える = something is visible TO you without effort; if YOU are making the effort to look, use <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る instead — think of it as 'it appears' vs. 'I look.'
